blocks:
Ayahs

أَلَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ (١)
Did We not open your breast for you (1)
وَوَضَعْنَا عَنكَ وِزْرَكَ (٢)
and relieve you of your burden (2)
ٱلَّذِىٓ أَنقَضَ ظَهْرَكَ (٣)
which [almost] broke your back? (3)
وَرَفَعْنَا لَكَ ذِكْرَكَ (٤)
Did We not exalt your name? (4)
فَإِنَّ مَعَ ٱلْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا (٥)
Indeed ease accompanies hardship. (5)
إِنَّ مَعَ ٱلْعُسْرِ يُسْرًا (٦)
Indeed ease accompanies hardship. (6)
فَإِذَا فَرَغْتَ فَٱنصَبْ (٧)
So when you are done, appoint, (7)
وَإِلَىٰ رَبِّكَ فَٱرْغَب (٨)
and supplicate your Lord. (8)

Content and Virtue of Surah Al-Inshirah

It is well-known that this Surah was revealed after Surah Ad-Duha, and its content confirms this fact, as it once again enumerates a portion of the divine favors bestowed upon the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله). In fact, three great favors were mentioned in Surah Ad-Duha, and three great favors are mentioned in Surah Al-Inshirah. Some of the previous favors were material and some spiritual, but the three favors in this Surah are all spiritual in nature. The core of this Surah revolves around three axes:

First, the explanation of these three blessings; second, glad tidings to the Prophet regarding the removal of obstacles in his mission in the future; and third, turning toward the One God and encouraging and inciting toward worship and supplication.

For this reason, as we have previously mentioned, in the narrations of the Ahl al-Bayt (عليه السلام), these two are considered as one Surah. Therefore, in the recitation of prayer (Salat), to ensure a complete Surah is recited, both are read together.

Among the Sunnis as well, some support this theory, as Fakhr al-Razi narrated from Tawus and Umar ibn Abd al-Aziz that they also said these two Surahs are a single Surah and would recite both in one Rak'ah, omitting the "Bismillah" between them (however, according to the fatwas of our jurists, "Bismillah" must be present in both, and what the late Tabarsi narrated in Majma' al-Bayan—that our jurists omit the "Bismillah"—does not seem correct).

Surprisingly, after mentioning the opinion of those who call them a single Surah, Fakhr al-Razi says this statement is incorrect because their contents differ.

Surah Ad-Duha was revealed when the Messenger of Allah was distressed by the harassment of the disbelievers and was living in hardship and sorrow, whereas the second was revealed when the Prophet was happy and joyful; how can these two be reconciled? [^1] However, this is a strange argument, because both Surahs speak of the Prophet's past life, at a time when he had left many problems behind and his pure heart was overflowing with hope and joy. Both Surahs speak of divine favors and remind him of the difficult and painful past to serve as a consolation for the Prophet's heart and to provide greater and more complete hope.

In any case, the close connection between the contents of these two Surahs is not something that can be doubted. A similar case will come in Surah Al-Fil and Quraysh, Insha'Allah. Regarding whether this Surah (Al-Inshirah) was revealed in Mecca or Medina, it is clear from the above statements that it was revealed in Mecca. However, considering the verse: "And We raised high for you your renown" (وَرَفَعْنَا لَكَ ذِكْرَكَ), some believe it was revealed in Medina, at a time when the fame of Islam and the Prophet had spread everywhere. But in fairness, this reason is not convincing, because the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), despite all the problems he faced in Mecca, was renowned, and in all gatherings, there was talk of his uprising, mission, and call. Through the annual Hajj gathering, this fame reached other regions of the Hijaz, especially Medina.

Regarding the virtue of reciting this Surah, we read in a Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) that he said:

"Whoever recites it will be given a reward like one who met Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله) while he was grieved and removed the sorrow from his heart." [^2]

[^1]: Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 32, p. 2. [^2]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 507.

Commentary

We showered you with various blessings

The tone of the verses is blended with the extraordinary grace and love of the Lord and the consolation and comfort of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله).

In the first verse, referring to the most important divine favor, He says: "Did We not expand for you your breast?" (أَ لَمْ نَشْرَحْ لَكَ صَدْرَكَ).

"Nashrah" is from the root sharah, which originally, according to Raghib in Al-Mufradat, means to expand pieces of meat and produce thinner slices. He then adds that the meaning of sharah al-sadr (expansion of the breast) is its expansion through divine light, tranquility, and God-given peace. He further states that "explaining" (sharah) the difficulties of speech means expanding it and clarifying hidden meanings. In any case, there is no doubt that the meaning of "expansion of the breast" here is metaphorical, referring to the expansion of the Prophet's soul and thought. This expansion can have a broad meaning that includes both the Prophet's scientific vastness through revelation and mission, and the expansion of his endurance and steadfastness against the stubbornness and sabotage of enemies and opponents.

Therefore, when Musa ibn Imran (peace be upon him) was commissioned to call the tyrannical Pharaoh—"Go to Pharaoh, for he has transgressed"—he immediately pleaded: "My Lord, expand for me my breast and ease for me my task" (Taha 25-26).

And elsewhere, addressing the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), it says: "So be patient for the decision of your Lord and be not like the companion of the fish [Jonah]"—now that it is so, wait for your Lord's command, be steadfast and patient, and do not be like Jonah (who, due to leaving the necessary patience, fell into all those problems and bitterness) (Al-Qalam 48).

Expansion of the breast is, in fact, the opposite of "constriction of the breast" (diq al-sadr), as we read in verse 97 of Surah Al-Hijr: "And We already know that your breast is constrained by what they say." Fundamentally, no great leader can go to battle against problems without expansion of the breast, and he whose mission is the greatest of all (like the Holy Prophet) must have the greatest expansion of the breast. Storms should not disturb the peace of the ocean of his soul; problems should not bring him to his knees; the sabotage of enemies should not make him despair; and questions about complex issues should not put him in a tight spot. This was the greatest divine gift to the Messenger of Allah.

Thus, it is mentioned in a Hadith that the Prophet said: "I made a request to my Lord, and I wish I had not made it. I said: 'O Lord, some of the prophets before me, You placed the flow of the wind at their disposal, and some would bring the dead to life.' God said to me: 'Were you not an orphan and I gave you refuge?' I said: 'Yes.' He said: 'Were you not lost and I guided you?' I said: 'Yes, O Lord!' He said: 'Did I not expand your breast and lighten your back?' I said: 'Yes, O Lord!'" [^1] This shows that the blessing of "expansion of the breast" is superior to the miracles of the prophets. Truly, if one studies the life of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) closely and observes the extent of his expansion of the breast during the difficult and complex events of his life, one becomes certain that this is not possible through ordinary means; it is a divine and Lordly support.

Here, some have said that the expansion of the breast refers to the event that occurred in the Prophet's childhood or youth, where the angels of heaven came and split his chest, took out his heart, washed it, and filled it with knowledge, wisdom, kindness, and mercy. [^2] It is obvious that the meaning of this Hadith is not this physical heart, but rather a metaphor and an indication of divine assistance in terms of the soul, strengthening the Prophet's will and determination, and purifying him from any moral defects and satanic whispers.

However, in any case, we have no reason to believe that the verse under discussion refers specifically to this incident; rather, it has a broad and vast meaning of which this story may be considered an instance.

And it was because of this expansion of the breast that the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) overcame the difficulties of the mission in the most sublime way and performed his duties on this path excellently.

[^1]: Majma' al-Bayan, Vol. 10, p. 508. [^2]: Tafsir Durr al-Manthur (as quoted in Tafsir al-Mizan, Vol. 20, p. 452) and Tafsir Fakhr al-Razi, Vol. 32, p. 2. The content of this Hadith also appears in Sahih Bukhari, Tirmidhi, and Nasa'i in the story of the Mi'raj.

Then, mentioning another of His great favors to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), He adds: "And We removed from you your burden," (وَ وَضَعْنا عَنْكَ وِزْرَكَ).

"Which weighed heavily upon your back," (الَّذِي أَنْقَضَ ظَهْرَكَ).

"Wizr" in the language means weight or heaviness. The word "Wazir" (minister) is also derived from this meaning because he carries the heavy burdens of government. Sins are also called "wizr" for this reason, as they are a heavy burden on the sinner's back.

"Anqada" is from the root naqd, meaning to undo a knot in a rope or to separate the compressed parts of a building. "Intiqad" refers to the sound heard when parts of a building separate from each other, or the sound of the vertebrae of the back when placed under a heavy load. This word is also used regarding the breaking of covenants and contracts, where it is said someone "broke" (naqada) the oath.

Thus, the above verse says: Allah removed that heavy and back-breaking burden from you.

What was this burden that Allah removed from His Prophet's back? The context of the verses clearly shows that it refers to the difficulties of the mission and prophethood, the call toward monotheism and the Oneness of God, and the eradication of the effects of corruption from that highly polluted environment. Not only the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) but all prophets faced such immense difficulties at the beginning of their call, and they only triumphed over them with divine assistance. However, the conditions of the environment and time of the Prophet of Islam (صلى الله عليه وآله) were harder and heavier in some respects.

Some have also interpreted "wizr" as the heavy burden of "revelation" at the beginning of its descent. Some as the misguidance, stubbornness, and enmity of the polytheists. Some as their extraordinary harassment and persecution. Some as the grief resulting from the death of his uncle Abu Talib and his wife Khadijah. And finally, some have interpreted it regarding the issue of infallibility and purity from sin.

But apparently, the first interpretation is the most appropriate of all, and these others are considered branches and leaves of it.

And in the third favor, He says: "And We raised high for you your renown" (وَ رَفَعْنا لَكَ ذِكْرَكَ). [^1] Your name spread everywhere along with Islam and the name of the Quran, and better yet, your name is mentioned alongside the name of Allah every morning and evening atop the minarets and during the Adhan. The testimony to your messengership, alongside the testimony to the Oneness and Unity of Allah, is the mark of Islam and the proof of accepting this pure religion.

What pride could be greater than this, and what elevation of status could be imagined higher than this?! In a Hadith from the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) regarding the interpretation of this verse, we read that he said:

"Gabriel said to me: 'Allah the Almighty says: Whenever My name is mentioned, your name is also mentioned along with Me' (and this suffices for the greatness of your status)."

The expression "for you" (laka) is an emphasis that He made the Prophet's name and renown high despite all those sabotages and enmities.

[^1]: The use of "raising" (raf') after "removing" (wad') has a special grace here, considering they are opposites.

A question arises here: this Surah was revealed in Mecca, while the spread of Islam, the resolution of the mission's difficulties, the removal of heavy burdens from the Prophet's shoulders, and the rising of his renown in the corners of the world occurred in Medina.

In response to this question, some have said: the meaning is that the glad tidings of it were given to the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) beforehand, and it was this that removed the burden of sorrow and grief from his heart. Sometimes it is said: the "past" tense here conveys the "future" meaning and is a promise regarding the future.

But the truth is that a part of these matters was realized in Mecca, especially in the latter part of the thirteen-year period when the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) was busy calling the people in Mecca. Faith penetrated the hearts of many, and difficulties became relatively fewer. The name and renown of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) spread everywhere, and the ground was prepared for the great victories of the future.

Interestingly, Hassan ibn Thabit, the famous poet of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), referred to the content of this verse in a beautiful form in his praise of the Prophet, saying:

And the Deity joined the Prophet's name to His own name, When the Mu'adhin says "I bear witness..." five times. And He derived for him a name from His own to honor him, So the Owner of the Throne is "Mahmud" and this is "Muhammad"!

In the next verse, He gives His Prophet the most important glad tidings and sprinkles the lights of hope upon his pure heart, saying: "For indeed, with hardship [will be] ease." (فَإِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً)

He emphasizes again: "Indeed, with hardship [will be] ease." (إِنَّ مَعَ الْعُسْرِ يُسْراً) Do not grieve; difficulties and hardships will not remain in this form. The sabotage of the enemies will not continue forever, and the material deprivations, economic problems, and poverty of the Muslims will not persist in this way.

He who endures difficulties and shows resistance against storms will one day taste its sweet fruit—a day when the cries of the enemies are silenced, the sabotages become colorless, the roads of progress and perfection become smooth, and traversing the path of truth becomes easy.

Although some commentators have considered these verses as referring to the general financial poverty of the Muslims at the beginning of the emergence of Islam, the breadth of the verses' concept includes all difficulties. These two verses are presented in a way that is not exclusive to the person of the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) and his time, but rather as a general rule and as a justification for the previous discussions. It gives hope to all sincere and striving believers that ease is always alongside hardship; it does not even use the expression "after" (ba'd), but rather "with" (ma'), which is a sign of accompaniment.

Yes, it is so: with every difficulty, ease is blended, and with every hardship, facility is accompanied, and these two have always been and will always be together.

This is a divine promise and glad tiding that gives light and purity to the heart, makes one hopeful of victories, and wipes the dust of despair and hopelessness from the page of the human soul. [^1] In a Hadith, it is mentioned that the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله) said:

"And know that with hardship is ease, and with patience is victory, and that relief is with distress." [^2] And in a Hadith from the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), we read: A woman brought a complaint against her husband to him that he does not spend anything on her, while her husband was truly destitute. Ali (عليه السلام) refrained from throwing her husband into prison and replied: "Indeed, with hardship is ease" (and invited her to patience and endurance). [^3]

Then, in the final verses of this Surah, He says: "So when you have finished [your duties], then labor [in worship]," (فَإِذا فَرَغْتَ فَانْصَبْ).

Never remain idle; do not set aside effort and striving. Be constantly engaged in struggle (mujahadah) and make the end of one important task the beginning of another.

"And to your Lord direct [your] longing." (وَ إِلى‌ رَبِّكَ فَارْغَبْ).

In all these states, rely on God and turn your attention toward your Lord. Seek His pleasure, search for His satisfaction, and hasten toward the proximity of His presence.

According to what has been said, the verse has a broad concept that includes finishing any important task and engaging in another, and it recommends directing all efforts toward the Lord. However, most commentators have mentioned limited meanings for the verse, each of which can be accepted as an instance:

A group has said the meaning is: when you have finished the obligatory prayer, engage in supplication and ask God to give you whatever you want. Or: when you have finished the obligatory duties, rise for the night prayer (Nafilah). Or: when you have finished with the world, engage in the affairs of the Hereafter and the worship and prayer of your Lord. Or: when you have finished the obligations, turn your attention to the recommended acts (Mustahabbat) that God has commanded. Or: when you have finished the Jihad against the enemy, rise for worship. Or: when you have finished the Jihad against the enemy, engage in the Jihad against the self (Jihad al-Nafs). Or: when you have finished delivering the mission, rise to request intercession!

In several narrations that the famous Sunni scholar Hafiz Hakim Haskani has narrated in Shawahid al-Tanzil, it is mentioned from Imam al-Sadiq (عليه السلام) that he said: it means "when you have finished [the mission], appoint Ali to the Wilayah." [^1] Qurtubi also narrated in his Tafsir from some that the meaning of the verse is "when you have finished, appoint the Imam who is your successor" (although he himself did not accept this meaning). [^2] Given that the subject of "finishing" is not specified in the noble verse, and "fansab" is from the root nasab, meaning fatigue and effort, the verse expresses a general and comprehensive principle. Its goal is to prevent the Prophet, as a role model and example, from engaging in rest after finishing an important matter, and to remind him of continuous and persistent effort in life.

With this meaning, it becomes clear that all the above interpretations are correct, but each as an instance of this comprehensive and general meaning.

And what a constructive and effective program it is, in which the secret of victory and perfection is hidden. Fundamentally, being idle and in complete leisure is a cause of fatigue, decreased vitality, laziness, and exhaustion, and in many cases, a cause of corruption, ruin, and various sins.

It is noteworthy that statistics show that during the holidays of educational institutions, the rate of corruption sometimes rises up to seven times! In any case, the entirety of this Surah expresses special divine care for the Holy Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), his consolation in the face of difficulties, and the promise of victory and support for him against the problems and ups and downs of the path of the mission.

At the same time, it is a hopeful, constructive, and life-giving collection for all human beings and all wayfarers on the path of truth.

[^1]: From what we have said, it is clear that the "Alif and Lam" in "Al-Usr" is for the genus, not for a specific reference, and the word "Yusr," although mentioned in the indefinite form, also conveys a generic meaning, and being indefinite in such cases is to express greatness. [^2] & [^3]: Tafsir Nur al-Thaqalayn, p. 604, Hadith 11 and 13.

Key Points

1- As we have pointed out above, it is mentioned in several narrations that the meaning of the verse "So when you have finished, then labor" (as an expression of an instance) is the appointment of the Commander of the Faithful, Ali (عليه السلام), to the Caliphate after performing the matter of the mission.

Alusi in Ruh al-Ma'ani, after quoting this statement from some of the "Imamiyyah," says: they read "fansib" with a kasrah on the "sad," and even if it were so, it would not be a reason that the intention is the appointment of Ali ibn Abi Talib. Then he quotes from Zamakhshari in Al-Kashshaf that if such an interpretation is possible for the Shi'a, the "Nasibis" (enemies of Ali (عليه السلام)) could also interpret it as a command to "nasb" (meaning hatred of Ali ibn Abi Talib)! [^1] Because "ansab" (with a fathah on the "sad") means to tire oneself and strive, while "ansib" (with a kasrah on the "sad") is a command to appoint, raise, and establish.

These commentators have imagined that the "Shi'a" change the recitation of the verse to argue for the issue of Wilayah, whereas there is never a need for such a change. Rather, this very well-known and recognized recitation is sufficient for the said interpretation, because it says: after finishing an important matter like the mission, strive and effort for another important matter like the Wilayah. This is perfectly acceptable as an instance, and we know that the Prophet, according to the famous Hadith of Ghadir and numerous other Hadiths found in the books of all Muslim scholars, had a persistent and continuous effort in this regard. But how offensive it is that a scholar like Zamakhshari, who considers Ali (عليه السلام) as the fourth great leader of Islam and the successor of the Prophet (صلى الله عليه وآله), allows himself to say that the Nasibis also have the right to interpret the verse as hatred of Ali ibn Abi Talib? What a crude and offensive expression? And that too from such a commentator?

Truly, what ugly consequences prejudice can have!

[^1]: Shawahid al-Tanzil, Vol. 2, p. 349 (Hadiths 1116 to 1119). [^2]: Qurtubi, Vol. 10, p. 7199. [^3]: Ruh al-Ma'ani, Vol. 30, p. 172; Tafsir al-Kashshaf, Vol. 4, p. 772.

[A Strange Statement from Ibn Abi al-Hadid]

2- The famous Mu'tazilite scholar Ibn Abi al-Hadid, in his commentary on Nahj al-Balagha, quotes from Zubayr ibn Bakkar—who, according to him, was neither a Shi'a nor an enemy of Mu'awiyah, but rather separated from Ali (عليه السلام), withdrew, and joined the opponents—who narrated from the son of Mughirah ibn Shu'bah: My father, Mughirah, used to speak much of Mu'awiyah's intellect and wisdom and admired his way of thinking. But one night he came from him while he was very sad and upset. I realized something important had happened. I asked him, and I saw him severely reviling Mu'awiyah. I asked the reason, and he said: "Tonight I was in private with him, and I said to him: 'You have reached the positions you wanted; now strive for justice and the spread of good deeds, for you have aged. Be kind to the Banu Hashim as well, for today they are not considered a threat to you, and this will be a cause of a good name for you.'"

He replied: "Alas! What name will remain of me? The first and second Caliphs did all those things; what name remains of them? But look at Ibn Abi Kabshah (Muhammad (صلى الله عليه وآله)), whose name is taken five times every day atop the minarets in the form of 'I bear witness that Muhammad is the Messenger of Allah.' O wretched one! What work remains after him? And what name will remain for us? No, by God, unless this situation changes and the Prophet's name is buried!" [^1]

But according to "And We raised high for you your renown," Allah has willed that this name be renowned throughout history and in the entire world of humanity, whether others like it or not, whether they are pleased or displeased. Fundamentally, if we analyze these expressions, what will they mean?! There is no power or strength except by Allah!

O Allah! Empty our hearts of self-love and fill them with Your love. O Lord! You Yourself have promised that with every difficulty there is ease and comfort; relieve the Muslims of today from these great difficulties and unpleasantries that reach them from the enemies. O God! Your blessings and favors upon us are many; grant us the success to be grateful for them.

Amen, O Lord of the Worlds. End of Surah Al-Inshirah.

[^1]: Sharh Nahj al-Balagha, Vol. 5, p. 129. Ibn Abi al-Hadid's phrase here is: "So what work remains? And what renown lasts after this? No father to you, no by Allah, except burial, burial!"
